name: Amrtananda-natha
dates_notes: Dated to the fourteenth century on the basis of his position in the Śrīvidyā lineage between Puṇyānanda (twelfth–thirteenth century, author of the Kāma-Kalā-Vilāsa) and the later Śrīvidyā commentators including Śivānanda Yogin and Lakṣmīdhara. The Yoginī-Hṛdaya-Dīpikā colophon and the citation chain through the Setubandha and the Saubhāgya-Bhāskara fix the date range. Modern scholarship (Padoux; Brooks; Goudriaan-Gupta) accepts the fourteenth-century placement.
core_thesis: Amṛtānandanātha is the principal fourteenth-century Śrīvidyā synthesist — the figure through whom the Kashmirian non-dual Śaiva-Śākta metaphysics is fully integrated into the South-Indian Śrīvidyā tradition of the worship of the goddess Lalitā-Tripurasundarī. His two major works are central. The *Dīpikā* on the *Yoginī-Hṛdaya* — Amṛtānanda's commentary on the second half of the *Vāmakeśvarī-Mata-Tantra* (the principal Śrīvidyā tantra; its two halves are the *Nityā-Ṣoḍaśikārṇava* and the *Yoginī-Hṛdaya*) — is the principal medieval commentary on the *Yoginī-Hṛdaya* and the document through which the Śrīvidyā's mantra-and-cakra cosmology (the *Śrī-Cakra* with its forty-three triangles, the *Śrī-Vidyā* with its fifteen syllables, the *Lalitā* with her retinue of fifteen *nityās* and their attendants) is articulated philosophically within a non-dual recognitional framework derived from the Pratyabhijñā and Krama traditions. The *Cidvilāsa-Stava* — Amṛtānanda's hymnic philosophical poem — articulates the metaphysics of consciousness as *cit-vilāsa* (the play of awareness): the universe is the playful self-manifestation of the goddess as the universal consciousness, and the *Śrī-Cakra* is the diagrammatic and ritual articulation of that self-manifestation. The synthesis is foundational for the developed Sarvāmnāya tradition because Amṛtānanda is the figure through whom (i) the Kashmirian non-dual Śaiva-Śākta metaphysics is welded to (ii) the South-Indian goddess-tantra ritual programme, with the result that (iii) the Śrīvidyā becomes the principal modern Sanskrit-pandit non-dual goddess tradition. Bhāskararāya in the eighteenth century writes his *Setubandha* commentary on the *Nityā-Ṣoḍaśikārṇava* and his *Saubhāgya-Bhāskara* on the *Lalitā-Sahasranāma* explicitly within the lineage Amṛtānanda founds.

engaged_works.title: Yogini-Hrdaya-Dipika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored by Amṛtānandanātha; standard medieval Śrīvidyā commentary on the Yoginī-Hṛdaya half of the Vāmakeśvarī-Mata-Tantra. Edited in the Sanskrit editions (Kashi Sanskrit Series; cf. Padoux's translation of the Yoginī-Hṛdaya with the Dīpikā).

ascription_evidence.description: Padoux's edition and French translation of the Yoginī-Hṛdaya with the Dīpikā (1994; English revised edition 2013 with R. Jeanty); standard reference in Brooks (*The Secret of the Three Cities*, 1990).

engaged_works.summary: A commentary on the *Yoginī-Hṛdaya* — the second half of the *Vāmakeśvarī-Mata-Tantra* — articulating the Śrīvidyā's mantra-and-cakra cosmology within a non-dual recognitional framework. The opening sections treat the *Śrī-Cakra*'s structural articulation (the central *bindu*; the inverted *trikoṇa*; the *aṣṭāra* of eight triangles; the *antar-daśāra* of ten inner triangles; the *bahir-daśāra* of ten outer triangles; the *caturdaśāra* of fourteen triangles; the *aṣṭa-dala-padma* of the eight-petalled lotus; the *ṣoḍaśa-dala-padma* of the sixteen-petalled lotus; the *bhūpura* of the three-fold square enclosure — forty-three triangles total). The middle sections treat the *Śrī-Vidyā* (the fifteen-syllable mantra of the goddess) and the *ṣoḍaśī* (the sixteen-syllable secret extension). The closing sections treat the *cakra-pūjā* programme — the ritual worship of the *nityā*-deities at each level of the *Śrī-Cakra* — and the recognitional soteriology in which the *Śrī-Cakra* is recognized as the diagrammatic articulation of the *sādhaka*'s own consciousness.

engaged_works.title: Cidvilasa-Stava
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored hymnic philosophical poem by Amṛtānandanātha; transmitted in the Śrīvidyā lineage as a foundational hymn.

ascription_evidence.description: Standard reference in Brooks and in the Sanskrit Śrīvidyā stava-collections.

engaged_works.summary: A hymnic philosophical poem articulating the metaphysics of consciousness as *cit-vilāsa* — the play of awareness. The universe is the playful self-manifestation of the goddess as universal consciousness; the *Śrī-Cakra* is the diagrammatic and ritual articulation of that self-manifestation. The hymn weaves together the recognitional soteriology of the Pratyabhijñā (the Kashmirian non-dual Śaiva inheritance), the Krama analysis of consciousness-as-sequence (the Mahānaya inheritance), and the Śrīvidyā ritual programme into a single devotional articulation. It is one of the principal Śrīvidyā stotra-works and is treated by the subsequent Śrīvidyā tradition (notably Bhāskararāya) as a foundational source for the school's self-understanding.

reception_notes: Amṛtānandanātha is the indispensable medieval figure through whom the Kashmirian non-dual Śaiva-Śākta metaphysics enters the developed South-Indian Śrīvidyā. The lineage Amṛtānanda → Śivānanda Yogin → Lakṣmīdhara → Bhāskararāya is the spine of the modern Śrīvidyā tradition. Modern scholarship — Padoux's translation of the Yoginī-Hṛdaya with the Dīpikā (1994/2013) and Brooks's *The Secret of the Three Cities* (1990) — establishes him as the principal medieval Śrīvidyā synthesist. The Sarvāmnāya integration tradition treats the Śrīvidyā as the principal modern living non-dual Śākta lineage, and Amṛtānanda is its founding theologian.
